title: DeepSeek V4 Built Natively on Huawei Ascend — First Frontier AI Model Without Nvidia
detail: The Information reported on April 3 (confirmed by Reuters on April 4) that DeepSeek's upcoming V4 frontier model is being developed to run natively on Huawei's Ascend AI chips, abandoning Nvidia hardware for the first time in the lab's history. DeepSeek worked directly with Huawei and Cambricon Technologies to rewrite portions of V4's training and inference code to optimize for Ascend architecture, bypassing the CUDA software ecosystem that had previously constrained Chinese AI labs to Nvidia hardware. DeepSeek withheld early V4 access from Nvidia and AMD while granting Huawei extended optimization time — a deliberate strategic pivot. The V4 model, expected to launch in the final two weeks of April 2026, represents two variants targeting different capability profiles. Major Chinese tech companies — Alibaba, ByteDance, and Tencent — placed bulk orders for hundreds of thousands of Huawei Ascend chips following the report, driving Ascend chip prices up approximately 20% within days. This marks a strategically defining milestone: for the first time, China's leading frontier AI lab is deploying a world-competitive model on entirely domestic semiconductor infrastructure, validating China's AI chip self-sufficiency strategy under the 15th Five-Year Plan.

title: Chinese Chip Firms Hit Records: CXMT Revenue Surges 130% YoY to $8B; US Controls Accelerate Domestic Demand
detail: CNBC reported on April 3 that China's leading chipmakers posted record revenues in 2025, driven by a combination of AI demand and US export controls forcing Chinese companies to source domestically. ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT), China's primary DRAM manufacturer, recorded 130% year-on-year revenue growth to approximately 55 billion yuan (~$8 billion) — a dramatic acceleration reflecting hyperscaler demand for domestically manufactured memory chips as US sanctions blocked access to Samsung and SK Hynix HBM for AI applications. SMIC, China's top foundry, confirmed 2025 revenue of $9.3 billion (+16% YoY), while also reporting growth in advanced packaging capacity for AI chip applications. TrendForce analysts attributed the surge partly to Chinese internet giants front-running anticipated future restrictions by filling domestic chip inventories. The results represent a significant inflection point in China's semiconductor import substitution strategy: while China still imports hundreds of billions in foreign chips annually, domestic chipmakers are now growing fast enough to be competitively relevant in specific market segments — particularly AI inference and DRAM — that were previously dominated by US-allied suppliers.
